speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I promise I'll come to the wedding. Then, next Friday-- it was -- I came-- I arrived Sunday, back to Jerusalem. And Friday, it was, in Jerusalem, Shabbat. The kids, everybody, is dressed nice. You're waiting outside. The master should come home for to make the kiddush, And, you know, they have the meal in on Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are sitting all about. The kids are waiting. The master should come home. All of a sudden, I see a-- a car stopped-- a taxi-- a taxi stopped. My husband popped out from there. I said, what's the matter? What are you doing here? He says, don't worry, I have my reservations in a hotel. I came to see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what he had in mind at that time. So, I said to him, well, if you-- as long as you have reservations in a hotel, then doesn't bother me. Because I am fifth-- like a fifth-- you say wheel by-- by the truck-- you know, a fifth wheel by anything. I says, I am alone here by you know-- by strange people, and I couldn't accept that you should be here. He says, no, no, I wouldn't dare do that. So-- but he came. He had with us Shabbat. He sat with us. I introduced him to the family. He is my friend. He-- thanks to him, I'm here. And after that, they had a daughter. She was my age. So we all three of us, we went out. You know, Jerusalem night, you don't drive, so we walked in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the next day, he came-- he went home. And this took-- this went on and on for three weeks, one week after the other, one week after the other. Then he said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his father said to him, what are you doing so-- you're wasting your time. You're going away. You're leaving your work. You're go away to Jerusalem. Why are you doing this? He says, well. He said, I met a girl, which I know her from camp. We met after the liberation. And I'm going there to see her. So he says, why don't you bring her out to Tel Aviv, so you don't have to drive every week to Jerusalem?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so he figured, maybe this is a good, you know-- a good idea. So he brought me, one day, to Tel Aviv, and I didn't know nothing what's going on. I was just so naive and so didn't believe that something is serious here. So I came to Tel Aviv. So he said-- the father says-- she can stay in our house, and you're going to sleep in the neighbor's house. And that's what happened. I came to Tel Aviv. I stayed there, and then we were going out more often. He was sleeping in the neighbor's house. We're going to go-- We used to go out more often then. So, he told me that, you probably don't know, but I am-- I am thinking really serious about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, well, I'm not decided yet to get married, or whatever. Give me-- give me time. I just want to just see, look around. What's going around my-- my world? But now, I'm here, I'm free. I can-- I can do everything what might heart desires. You know, I want to work. I want to just get myself a little bit dressed up and save up some money, whatever. He says, don't worry, we're going to  do both together.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to make the story short, he made me agree to-- I liked him already, at that time, you see. And it took us probably about three, four months. We got married in Israel, in Tel Aviv-- in Tel Aviv. And then, you know, after we got-- got there, my father-in-law bought us-- rent us an apartment. He bought us furniture, and he gave us, you know-- he helped us get a little bit situated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I was married. My brother, I took him out from school, and he stayed with us. And then, I got-- it was a year later, I got pregnant. I got a baby. My-- my husband went to the Army at that time, when I had the baby. In 1948, my son was born. And he was in the Army. And I went through a hard time in Israel, alone, by myself with the baby, and until he was two years in the Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, after he came back from the Army, we had to start again. We had to build our life. We had already a baby, so we have already a family. And it was a little bit harder then. It was not easy, because in Israel, at that time, it was not easy to get-- you know, he had a truck, and he worked, and he made money. But we had an apartment right there, and then we bought a house in Israel. And in 1948, my son was born. I was running with my son under the bullets from the Arabs. Went through very hard times. We were-- we lived in a house, where not far away from our house was an Arabic village. And the Arabs, start-- they shoot. They were throwing bombs from there-- the fire bombs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I saw this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, I didn't have my son yet. It was 1948. At that time, I was pregnant. And I said to my husband, look what's going on outside. Are we going to wait until they're going to bomb our house too? Let's go. Where are you going to go? Let's go to Tel Aviv. We lived in the surrounding Tel Aviv. So where we came close to Tel Aviv, the English Army was standing, like in a row, with tanks. They didn't let us through. My husband had to carry me-- me on his arms because I was not able to walk that far. I was the last month in pregnancy. They didn't let us go through. He says, why? Where are we going? We're here. We live here. You see my-- my-- my wife? I have to take her to the hospital. He just pretended. I have to take her to the hospital. They let us through.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through, the-- like they say, the border of the tanks. We went to Tel Aviv, and I stayed with my friend, my girlfriend. She was married by that time. I stayed with her until the whole thing, you know-- because the houses were burning. You know, close to us, the houses were burning, you know. And we-- nobody could stop the fires, because they-- they were continuing shooting, and shooting, and throwing the bombs. And that's what happened then. We lived there. And then, from there, we move to another house. We bought another house out of Tel Aviv, far away. I had my baby already by that time. I wanted him to grow up not in a city where the cars, and the-- and the smog, and everything is around. We moved out from out of the city in a place where it's a beautiful green, and bushes, and trees, that he should be able to-- I should be able to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- so we moved to another place that my baby could be able, you know, to-- to play outside. I could be able to go walk out with him, walk over there. Over there, you can walk in the middle of the night. You know, nobody will bother you, but because of the traffic, you know. In that time, it was right after, you know, the war. We were afraid of the Arabs. They-- they did a lot of, you know, crimes. They, you know, just got into-- they just broke into the-- the houses by the people, and they shoot. And they did a lot of damage to everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were very frightened. And then, again, a war. And I was so frustrated from the wars, and going through, again, the same thing. What we went through at camp is not enough. Then we had to-- we had to go through this in Israel too. So my husband said to me one day, you know what? I am tired already of going to the Army. He went through two wars, you know. He says, fine, I went through two wars, and I am still alive. But who knows? He was laying-- they were hiding themselves next to him for months. Hiding next to him from the other side. People were dying. They were killed, you know, from the bullets of the Arabs. So, he said, one war I went through, and the other war, and the next one, I went through. But who knows if I'm going to-- I'm going to survive another war now? I would like to leave this country, because this is already a little bit too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through too much in life. We have to make a life for ourselves that we should go to sleep, and sleep quieted. Not tomorrow there's going to be another war, or-- or you hear the-- the shooting from the Arabs. Well, matter of fact, at that time, my sister was already in the United States. She sent me a preference. I should be able to go to America-- but by myself. And I had already at that time, when we lived-- I had already two children by that time. So, I says to him-- I told-- my husband, said, go. I'll be with the kids. I said, no. If we're going to go-- we're going to emigrate to the United States-- we're going to go together. If not, we're going to stay together. I'm not leaving my kids. I don't want nobody should take care of my kids. I'm their mother, and it's my responsibility.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how-- that is what I was. I didn't go. Other people did go-- mother's who left the kids, and they went to the United States. But I didn't. Then it didn't take long-- but probably two, two, three years-- it was not long, right? It came out, a law, that if somebody has a sister in  the United States, can come with the whole family. So that time, we start working on the papers. We came to the United States. We liquidated everything there. We sold everything out, sold our house. We came to the States here. And then we had to start, again, a new life.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband went to work in the beginning. We brought some money from there, because we sold everything we had there. We brought some money. We couldn't bring the money in there, but we got the money slowly-- you know, small amounts of money. We got it in. And my husband said, I'm not going to do with the money nothing. We'll leave the money in the bank, and I'm going to go to work. And we'll see what we can do in the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, he went to work. He went to work to Best Kosher, to the factory where they're making salamis. He worked over there with a lot of Germans. You can imagine how he felt there. So, he worked. He was cutting meat, and with a bone-- the bone got in his skin here. He got an infection. He got sick. They fired him. They were afraid he's going to make a case, so they fired him. But, in the beginning, he didn't want to lose the job, so he went to work even with a sick arm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those German guys helped him. They felt, somehow, guilty. He told them that he is from the camps. And he told them what he went through in Germany. But those Germans, probably, they're here, American Germans. So they felt-- they pitied him a little bit, and they helped him. They helped him, you know. But later on, the-- the factory-- the-- the boss fired him, because he was afraid he was going to make a big-- big deal out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, my husband, after that, he lost his job. He went to work for an insurance company-- a life insurance company-- again. He worked in what kind of-- they gave him, you know, the neighborhoods by the-- by the schwartzes, you know. One day, my cousin said to my husband, did you hear? Last-- last evening, they killed an agent-- an insurance agent. They killed him. So you want to get killed? You came from the camps. You're going to get killed again, now? Are you going to get killed? I want you to stop this job. Don't go anymore to this work. Quit this job. Let somebody else take over.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband made-- made very well in the insurance company. He was, every day, on the blackboard as the first-- the best salesman, the best sales. But I said, well, money is not everything. Let's do something productive for us. We had our money. We bought a market-- a small market. Then I came into the picture, and I started working with him. I helped him. It was also not in a good neighborhood in Chicago-- very bad neighborhood. But they didn't bother us that time. All of those-- the one-- the guys who used to make those hold-ups came into our store and said, oh, last night I held up here. Last night I-- the other night I, held up them. So my husband says, as long as you're not going to hold up me, it's OK. No, but they didn't bother us. It was a very, very dangerous neighborhood. Matter of fact, the police-- one day, they broke in in our store. They didn't make a hold-up, but they broke in the store. They took out half store of the merchandise there. The police came to my husband. He said, if you're not going to get out from there, they're going to kill you there too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, what could you do? We didn't have anything better for-- at that time. So my husband says, well, we're going to get rid of the store. But we have to take a little bit of time. We have to see what we should do next now. So we sold this market, and we bought a market-- a bigger supermarket on another street, in a better neighborhood, where white people are, not too many African-Americans. They didn't bother us anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what happened. One day, we had a fire in the store [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the refrigeration, from the cooler. They notified us that there's this fire there, and everything was gone. Luckily, we had insurance-- American insurance, We had insurance-- Lloyd of England. This was their insurance. We got some money from there. We built up, again the store. We open up again the store with a big, you know, with celebration, with everything, with we-- The customers came in, and we served them with wine, with-- with hors d'oeuvres, and everything. We were very happy that we could open-- reopen the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we were already here. Then-- then what happened next? Then, yeah, we had the market.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- I felt bad. I got sick. I went to the doctor. The doctor said that something wrong with my heart. I cannot stay in this city because it's too cold for me. I went once to a bar mitzvah, and I crossed the street, you know, and I couldn't catch up-- couldn't catch my breath. So the doctor says, or you go back to Israel or you go to California. Because in California, it's a warmer climate, and over there, it's going to be much better to you-- for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to California. Start again. See how many times we start again?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to California, to Los Angeles. I have a lot of, you know, my people from my-- from my hometown. But everybody gave us a good idea, but nobody give us a hand, you know, with something. But we didn't ask for anything, because we came. We had some money. We came. I went right away to work. I worked in a bakery. My husband went also to work. We both worked. We sent our kids to school. My son went to college. My daughter went to school. And then we start our life again. Then I had to go for surgery. I had to quit my job. I had to go to surgery. I had heart surgery at that time. I thought-- it was 1967. I was the first one, I probably think, that went through this kind of surgery. I didn't have open heart surgery from here, but I was cut here. I was sick for a whole year. I was just so sick that I felt I will never recuperate from this surgery. I will always be like that, I said. But time is the best healer. It took time. I got back on my feet, and I went back to work. Then I went to work, already, to a clothing store. I worked in a clothing store, and I was very happy. I made good money, and we bought a house. And then we lived in the house on Orlando. It was a duplex. We had a tenant upstairs. Then my husband decided, let's do more-- something more productive.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you know what? By then, we had already a building-- bought a building, a ten-unit building. He says, you know what? We have a vacancy. Let's move out. We're going to tear down the duplex. We're going to build a new building for ourselves-- a beautiful apartment in the front. And in the back, we're going to have tenants.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what we did. We lived a year in the apartment. And we built a building, and we had that-- we moved in at that time. And my son got married from there. From this house-- from Orlando, the duplex-- he got married. And then he moved out from the house. Only my daughter was with us. So I didn't feel-- I didn't have the feeling before, how is, you know, when a child moved out from the house, because we are not used to that, you know. But he was in the house until he got married. The day he went to the-- to the temple, to the wedding, he drove with us the limousine to the temple. And then my daughter starts going out with a guy. And it took a few years. She got married. But we were already moved into the house on Orlando. In the new house, we were very happy there. And the kids used to come, you know, to visit us-- the grandchildren and the children. And it was a happy life. We were a very happy family. I love my grandchildren dearly. And I love-- I love my children. Everybody loves their children. But I love more, even, the grandchildren. Yes, I can tell you. If you're going to have grandchildren, you're going to love them more than the children. Now they are on the first list by us. On the list, they're the first ones. If you're going to have sometimes grandchildren-- I had my granddaughter yesterday here. She lives in Grass Valley, and she is accepted in Santa Barbara, in college. And she went for-- for-- for a few days there with her mother to see how it's going to work out for her in school. She was here, so I was the happiest in life, that she was here with me. And so is the other one. I have an older one already, a 20-year-old one. Morris has already a son, 20 years old. And he comes over every time he has a chance. When he picks up the phone, he calls me and says, hi, Savta Shayna. This is his signal, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what can I add more to that? That we are a very-- we are a happy family. I have a very good husband. I am like a queen to him. And we love each other very much, and we have a very happy marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been married for 48 years. It's going on for 49, already, pretty soon. And we're looking forward to celebrate our 50 years anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, Mrs. Shapow, now that it's almost 50 years since the war, do you have any kind of a message that you'd like to relay to your children, your grandchildren, the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a message for my children, that I wish them that they would never go through what I went through, and so with my grandchildren, the same thing. My grandchildren are very interested in-- in the life we passed through. Because my grandson, matter of fact, is going to Adat Ari El-- my daughter's son-- a Hebrew school. And he interviewed us once. in school. They had an orientation in school. So he interviewed us, and he wrote a whole article about it. And he always thinks about it, because his two grandparents-- from my daughter's side and from my son-in-law's side-- are people from the Holocaust. We went through a Holocaust, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is what is the message to my children. They shouldn't-- they shouldn't go through this, what we went through. And they should have a good life, and their children should have a good life. That's all I can say, because, I hope that it's not going to recover a Hitler like used to be-- that this is not going to happen anymore. This is what I want to say-- that nobody should go through what we went through, that the world shouldn't let something happen like this happened. Thank you. It's a cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father and my mother. This is the picture where they were engaged. And they very young. Matter of fact, my father saw my mother, he fell in love with her. Really, she was very pretty, and she was very young. And, what can I say? He was-- my father was a very, very intelligent man. He was-- he spoke quite a few languages. He spoke German. He spoke Russian. He spoke Polish. He spoke quite a few languages. And everybody in the city loved him. He was a very-- everybody loved him, really. He was very famous in the city-- in my hometown. From the left, -- that's myself In the middle is my sister. She just came from Johannesburg. We had a reunion in Israel. She came to see us. And this is-- from the right-- is my brother. We all three got together in Israel. We wanted to see each other, because my sister is the oldest, and she's not a well woman. She's not-- she's sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we made the reunion that we should be able to see. For me, it's very hard, you know, to go to Johannesburg because the flight is a very hard flight, you know. And it takes a long time. It takes probably more than 30 hours to get there. So we decided that we're going to meet in Israel. And for her, from Israel to-- from Johannesburg to Israel is nine hours only. So that's why, we just-- that was a month ago, taking the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the top line, from the right side, is my son-in-law. Next to him is my daughter-in-law. Next to my daughter-in-law is my son, and next to my son is my daughter. The middle row is, from the left side, is my grandson, Varon, my granddaughter, Reniti. And the other boy is my daughter-in-law's son, Roy. To the right, the lower row is Jonathan. He's also my daughter-in-law's son. And this is myself. In the middle is my daughter's oldest son, Joshua. And next to Joshua is my husband, Nathan.
